88	## 3. The Two-Layer World Model
    89	
    90	The architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and
    91	sharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**
    92	
    93	### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)
    94	
    95	A finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of
    96	sight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.
    97	It is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable
    98	offline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a
    99	runtime dependency for taking the next turn.
   100	
   101	### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)
   102	
   103	An LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content
   104	language (the "floor manifest"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,
   105	NPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The
   106	Director reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.
   107	
   108	Every manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest
   109	that fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a
   110	broken floor, only a slightly less personal one.
   111	
   112	### The freedom spectrum
   113	
   114	"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom
   115	lives:
   116	
   117	| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |
   118	|---|---|
   119	| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |
   120	| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |
   121	| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |
   122	| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |
   123	| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |
   124	| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |
   125	
   126	The deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded
   127	invention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and
   128	behavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,
   129	yields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is
   130	something the engine already knows how to run.
   131	
   132	---
   133	
   134	## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)
   135	
   136	1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed
   137	   maximum depth per run.
   138	2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no
   139	   free-text gameplay commands.
   140	3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +
   141	   identical actions = identical run.
   142	4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and
   143	   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.
   144	5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI
   145	   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.
   146	6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,
   147	   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.
   148	7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing
   149	   the validation gauntlet.
   150	8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds
   151	   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.
   152	
   153	---
   154
